children.name: Vireo
children.description: Typical vireos — small, insectivorous songbirds of the Americas. Their name derives from Latin for 'I am green' — most species are olive-green above and pale below. Vireos are deliberate foragers, carefully inspecting leaves and branches for caterpillars and insects. They are better known for their persistent, repetitive songs than their subdued plumage.

children.name: Vireo olivaceus
children.description: One of the most common songbirds of North American deciduous forests. Olive-green above, white below, with a striking red eye and a grey crown bordered by black. Its persistent, monotonous song — a series of short phrases separated by pauses — can continue all day in summer. Longest song of any North American bird.

children.name: Vireo flavifrons
children.description: A striking vireo with a bright yellow throat and breast, white wing bars, and yellow spectacles. Found in open deciduous woodlands of eastern North America. Its song is a slow, burry series of three-note phrases.

children.name: Vireo gilvus
children.description: A plain, greyish vireo with no wing bars and a pale supercilium. Its song is a cheerful, rolling warble that seems to run on for ever. Found in open deciduous and riparian woodlands. It was the first bird ever recorded to lay a blue egg.

children.name: Vireo bellii
children.description: A small, greyish vireo of shrubby habitats in the central and southwestern United States and Mexico. One of the plainest vireos, lacking wing bars. Its song is a rapid, jumbled series of notes. The Least Bell's Vireo subspecies is Endangered.

children.name: Vireo griseus
children.description: A distinctive vireo with a white eye, yellow spectacles, and yellow flanks. Found in dense thickets and scrubby habitats in the eastern United States. Its explosive song begins with a sharp 'chick' before breaking into a complex series of notes. More often heard than seen.

children.name: Cyclarhis
children.description: Peppershrikes — stocky, large-billed vireos of Central and South America. Named for their preference for eating pepper berries and other fruit, which they crush with their thick, hooked bills. Their loud, ringing songs are a characteristic sound of Neotropical forest edges.

children.name: Cyclarhis gujanensis
children.description: A distinctive vireonid with a bold rufous eyebrow, grey crown, and yellow throat. Found from Mexico to Argentina. Its loud, ringing song of three to four notes is one of the most characteristic sounds of Neotropical forests. Feeds on fruit and large insects.
